/-- Proposition 9.8.3 / Exercise 9.8.4 -/
theorem Chapter9.MonotoneOn.exist_inverse
  {a b : ℝ} (h : a < b) (f : ℝ → ℝ)
  (hcont : ContinuousOn f (Set.Icc a b))
  (hmono : StrictMonoOn f (Set.Icc a b)) :
  f '' Set.Icc a b = Set.Icc (f a) (f b) ∧
    ∃ finv : ℝ → ℝ,
      ContinuousOn finv (Set.Icc (f a) (f b)) ∧
      StrictMonoOn finv (Set.Icc (f a) (f b)) ∧
      finv '' Set.Icc (f a) (f b) = Set.Icc a b ∧
      (∀ x ∈ Set.Icc a b, finv (f x) = x) ∧
      ∀ y ∈ Set.Icc (f a) (f b), f (finv y) = y := by
  sorry

end TaoBench

/--
Tao's theorem 11.2.16(d) for piecewise-constant functions, translated to Mathlib:

- The bounded interval `I : BoundedInterval` is represented by a closed interval
  `Icc a b : Set ℝ`.
- “`f` is piecewise constant on `I` with respect to a finite partition” is
  expressed by saying that the restricted map `fun x : Icc a b ↦ f x` has
  finite range.
- Tao's combinatorial integral of a piecewise-constant function over `I`
  is identified with the Lebesgue integral of `f` with respect to the
  restriction of `volume` to `Icc a b`.
- The conclusion is that this integral is nonnegative whenever `f ≥ 0` on `I`.
-/
theorem PiecewiseConstantOn.integ_of_nonneg
  {f : ℝ → ℝ} {a b : ℝ}
  (h : ∀ x ∈ Icc a b, 0 ≤ f x)
  (hf : (Set.range fun x : Icc a b ↦ f x).Finite) :
  0 ≤
    MeasureTheory.integral
      (MeasureTheory.Measure.restrict
        (MeasureTheory.volume : MeasureTheory.Measure ℝ)
        (Icc a b))
      f := by
  sorry

end TaoBench

/-- Proposition 9.3.14 (Limit laws for functions) / Exercise 9.3.2
in Mathlib language: if `x₀` is an adherent point of `E ⊆ ℝ`, and
`f` and `g` both converge along `E` to `L` and `M` respectively, then
`min f g` converges along `E` to `min L M`. -/
theorem Convergesto.min
  {E : Set ℝ} {f g : ℝ → ℝ} {L M : ℝ} {x₀ : ℝ}
  (h  : x₀ ∈ closure E)
  (hf : Tendsto f (nhdsWithin x₀ E) (𝓝 L))
  (hg : Tendsto g (nhdsWithin x₀ E) (𝓝 M)) :
  Tendsto (min f g) (nhdsWithin x₀ E) (𝓝 (min L M)) := by
  sorry

end TaoBench

/-- Exercise 5.2.2 (Mathlib-only formulation).

If two rational sequences `a, b : ℕ → ℚ` are eventually ε-close, i.e.
there exists `N` such that for all `n ≥ N` we have `|a n - b n| ≤ ε`,
then `a` is bounded iff `b` is bounded (where bounded means
`∃ M ≥ 0, ∀ n, |a n| ≤ M`). -/
theorem Sequence.isBounded_of_eventuallyClose
  {ε : ℚ} {a b : ℕ → ℚ}
  (hab : ∃ N : ℕ, ∀ ⦃n : ℕ⦄, n ≥ N → |a n - b n| ≤ ε) :
  (∃ M : ℚ, 0 ≤ M ∧ ∀ n : ℕ, |a n| ≤ M) ↔
  (∃ M : ℚ, 0 ≤ M ∧ ∀ n : ℕ, |b n| ≤ M) := by
  sorry

end Chapter5

end TaoBench

/-- Proposition 6.6.5 / Exercise 6.6.4 (Mathlib formulation).
A real sequence `a : ℕ → ℝ` converges to `L` if and only if every subsequence
(obtained by composing with a strictly increasing `f : ℕ → ℕ`) also converges to `L`. -/
theorem Chapter6.Sequence.convergent_iff_subseq (a : ℕ → ℝ) (L : ℝ) :
    Tendsto a atTop (𝓝 L) ↔
      ∀ b : ℕ → ℝ,
        (∃ f : ℕ → ℕ, StrictMono f ∧ ∀ n, b n = a (f n)) →
          Tendsto b atTop (𝓝 L) :=
  by sorry

end TaoBench

  atTop.limsup (fun n ↦ (((c n) ^ (1 / (n : ℝ)) : ℝ) : EReal)) ≤

theorem Convergesto.div
  {E : Set ℝ} {f g : ℝ → ℝ} {L M x₀ : ℝ}
  (hM : M ≠ 0)
  (hf :

  (hg :

  ∀ ε > 0, ∃ δ > 0,
    ∀ x ∈ E ∩ Ioo (x₀ - δ) (x₀ + δ),
      |f x / g x - L / M| < ε := by
  sorry

/--
Lemma 11.2.8 / Exercise 11.2.2 (piecewise constant functions).

Here `I : Set ℝ` is assumed to be a bounded interval of one of the
four standard types `Ioo`, `Icc`, `Ioc`, `Ico`. A function `f : ℝ → ℝ`
is *piecewise constant on* `I` if there exists a finite family `P` of
bounded intervals which

* are all contained in `I`,
* partition `I` (each `x ∈ I` lies in exactly one member of `P`), and
* on each member of `P` the function `f` is constant.

The statement is that if `f` and `g` are piecewise constant on `I`
in this sense, then so is `x ↦ max (f x) (g x)`.
-/
theorem PiecewiseConstantOn.max
  {f g : ℝ → ℝ} {I : Set ℝ}
  (hI :
    ∃ a b : ℝ,
      I = Set.Ioo a b ∨ I = Set.Icc a b ∨ I = Set.Ioc a b ∨ I = Set.Ico a b)
  (hf :
    ∃ P : Finset (Set ℝ),
      (∀ J ∈ P,
        ∃ a b : ℝ,
          J = Set.Ioo a b ∨ J = Set.Icc a b ∨ J = Set.Ioc a b ∨ J = Set.Ico a b) ∧
      (∀ x ∈ I, ∃! J : Set ℝ, J ∈ P ∧ x ∈ J) ∧
      (∀ J ∈ P, J ⊆ I) ∧
      (∀ J ∈ P, ∃ c : ℝ, ∀ x ∈ J, f x = c))
  (hg :
    ∃ P : Finset (Set ℝ),
      (∀ J ∈ P,
        ∃ a b : ℝ,
          J = Set.Ioo a b ∨ J = Set.Icc a b ∨ J = Set.Ioc a b ∨ J = Set.Ico a b) ∧
      (∀ x ∈ I, ∃! J : Set ℝ, J ∈ P ∧ x ∈ J) ∧
      (∀ J ∈ P, J ⊆ I) ∧
      (∀ J ∈ P, ∃ c : ℝ, ∀ x ∈ J, g x = c)) :
  ∃ P : Finset (Set ℝ),
    (∀ J ∈ P,
      ∃ a b : ℝ,
        J = Set.Ioo a b ∨ J = Set.Icc a b ∨ J = Set.Ioc a b ∨ J = Set.Ico a b) ∧
    (∀ x ∈ I, ∃! J : Set ℝ, J ∈ P ∧ x ∈ J) ∧
    (∀ J ∈ P, J ⊆ I) ∧
    (∀ J ∈ P, ∃ c : ℝ, ∀ x ∈ J, max (f x) (g x) = c) :=
by
  sorry

end TaoBench

  (hI : ∃ a b : ℝ,

/--
In Tao's construction, `Real` is defined as a quotient of Cauchy sequences of rationals
and `LIM` picks out the real number represented by a (Cauchy) rational sequence,
defaulting to `0` if the sequence is not Cauchy. The exercise

`Real.LIM.harmonic : LIM (fun n ↦ 1/((n:ℚ)+1)) = 0`

says that the rational sequence `a n = 1 / (n + 1)` converges to `0` in the
constructed real numbers.

In Mathlib, limits of sequences are expressed via `Tendsto` and filters.
The following theorem is the corresponding statement in standard Mathlib terms:
the real sequence `n ↦ 1 / (n + 1)` tends to `0` as `n → ∞`.
-/
theorem Real.LIM.harmonic :
    Tendsto (fun n : ℕ => (1 : ℝ) / ((n : ℝ) + 1)) atTop (𝓝 0) := by
  sorry

end TaoBench

If a real series `∑ a n` converges but not absolutely, then neither the
subseries of nonnegative terms nor the subseries of negative terms can be
made absolutely convergent by any bijective reindexing of those terms. -/

          Summable (fun k => |a (g k)|)) ∧

open Classical in
/-- Exercise 8.3.2 (Schröder–Bernstein lemma, set version).
Some subtle type changes due to how sets are implemented in Mathlib. Also we shift the
sequence `D` by one so that we can work in `Set A` rather than `Set B`. -/
theorem Schroder_Bernstein_lemma {X : Type} {A B C : Set X}
    (hAB : A ⊆ B) (hBC : B ⊆ C) (f : C ↪ A) :
  let D : ℕ → Set A :=
    Nat.rec
      ((f.image ∘ (B.embeddingOfSubset _ hBC).image)
        {x : B | (x : X) ∉ A})
      (fun _ =>
        (f.image ∘ (B.embeddingOfSubset _ hBC).image ∘ (A.embeddingOfSubset _ hAB).image))
  Set.univ.PairwiseDisjoint D ∧
    let g : A → B :=
      fun x =>
        if h : x ∈ ⋃ n, D n ∧ ∃ y : B, f ⟨(y : X), hBC y.property⟩ = x then
          h.2.choose
        else
          ⟨(x : X), hAB x.property⟩
    Function.Bijective g := by
  sorry

end TaoBench

`hf` and `hg` say that `f` and `g` are piecewise constant on the bounded interval

      (∀ s ∈ Q, s ⊆ Set.Icc a b) ∧
      (∀ s ∈ Q, ∃ c : ℝ, ∀ x ∈ s, g x = c)) :

namespace PiecewiseConstantWith

/--
Mathlib-style reformulation of Tao's Proposition 11.2.13:

Let `f : ℝ → ℝ` and let `[a, b]` be a closed, bounded interval.
Suppose we have two finite partitions `P` and `P'` of `[a, b]` into
(sub)intervals `[J.1, J.2]` (encoded as pairs `J : ℝ × ℝ`), such that:

* every point of `[a, b]` lies in a unique subinterval of `P`, and likewise for `P'`;
* on each subinterval of `P` (resp. `P'`), the function `f` is constant with
  value `cP J` (resp. `cP' J`).

Then the sum over the partition of “(constant value on the piece) × (length of the
piece)” is independent of the chosen partition.
-/
theorem integ_eq
  {f : ℝ → ℝ} {a b : ℝ} (h_ab : a ≤ b)
  (P P' : Finset (ℝ × ℝ))
  (cP cP' : (ℝ × ℝ) → ℝ)
  (hPcov :
    ∀ x, x ∈ Set.Icc a b →
      ∃! J : ℝ × ℝ, J ∈ P ∧ x ∈ Set.Icc J.1 J.2)
  (hP'cov :
    ∀ x, x ∈ Set.Icc a b →
      ∃! J : ℝ × ℝ, J ∈ P' ∧ x ∈ Set.Icc J.1 J.2)
  (hPconst :
    ∀ J ∈ P, ∀ x ∈ Set.Icc J.1 J.2, f x = cP J)
  (hP'const :
    ∀ J ∈ P', ∀ x ∈ Set.Icc J.1 J.2, f x = cP' J) :
  ∑ J ∈ P, cP J * max (J.2 - J.1) 0 =
  ∑ J ∈ P', cP' J * max (J.2 - J.1) 0 := by
  sorry

end PiecewiseConstantWith

end TaoBench

namespace TaoBench

 theorem upper_integ_le_upper_sum
    {f : ℝ → ℝ} {I : Set ℝ}
    (hf : ∃ M : ℝ, ∀ x ∈ I, |f x| ≤ M)
    (hI : ∃ a b : ℝ,
      I = Set.Icc a b ∨ I = Set.Ioc a b ∨ I = Set.Ico a b ∨ I = Set.Ioo a b)
    (P : Finset (Set ℝ))
    (hP_exists_unique : ∀ x ∈ I, ∃! J : Set ℝ, J ∈ P ∧ x ∈ J)
    (hP_subset : ∀ J ∈ P, J ⊆ I)
    (hP_intervals : ∀ J ∈ P, ∃ a b : ℝ,
      J = Set.Icc a b ∨ J = Set.Ioc a b ∨ J = Set.Ico a b ∨ J = Set.Ioo a b) :
    sInf
        ((fun g : ℝ → ℝ =>
            MeasureTheory.integral (MeasureTheory.volume.restrict I) g) ''
          {g : ℝ → ℝ |
            (∀ x ∈ I, f x ≤ g x) ∧
              (∃ Q : Finset (Set ℝ),
                (∀ x ∈ I, ∃! J : Set ℝ, J ∈ Q ∧ x ∈ J) ∧
                  (∀ J ∈ Q, J ⊆ I) ∧
                    (∀ J ∈ Q, ∃ c : ℝ, ∀ x ∈ J, g x = c) ∧
                    (∀ J ∈ Q, ∃ a b : ℝ,
                      J = Set.Icc a b ∨ J = Set.Ioc a b ∨ J = Set.Ico a b ∨
                        J = Set.Ioo a b))})
      ≤
      Finset.sum P (fun J => (sSup (f '' J)) * (MeasureTheory.volume J).toReal) := by
  sorry

end TaoBench

theorem upper_riemann_sum_le
  {f g : ℝ → ℝ} {a b : ℝ}
  (n : ℕ)
  (pts : ℕ → ℝ)
  (h_mono : StrictMono pts)
  (h0 : pts 0 = a)
  (hn : pts n = b)
  (hf : ∃ M : ℝ, ∀ x ∈ Icc a b, |f x| ≤ M)
  (hgf : ∀ x ∈ Icc a b, f x ≤ g x)
  (hg : ∃ (m : ℕ) (q : ℕ → ℝ)
            (hq_mono : StrictMono q)
            (hq0 : q 0 = a)
            (hqm : q m = b)
            (c : ℕ → ℝ),
          ∀ (k : ℕ), k < m → ∀ x, x ∈ Icc (q k) (q (k+1)) → g x = c k)
  :
  Finset.sum (Finset.range n)
    (fun k =>
      (sSup (f '' Icc (pts k) (pts (k+1)))) *
        ((pts (k+1)) - (pts k)))
  ≤
  MeasureTheory.integral
    (MeasureTheory.Measure.restrict MeasureTheory.volume (Icc a b)) g := by
  sorry

end TaoBench

/-- Exercise 11.3.2 (translated to Mathlib).

If `f` majorizes `g` on a bounded interval `I` (i.e. `g x ≤ f x` for all `x ∈ I`),
then `f + h` majorizes `g + h` on the same interval. Here a “bounded interval”
is any set of the form `Ioo a b`, `Icc a b`, `Ioc a b`, or `Ico a b`. -/
def MajorizesOn.of_add :
    Decidable
      (∀ (f g h : ℝ → ℝ) (I : Set ℝ),
        (∃ a b : ℝ,
          I = Set.Ioo a b ∨
            I = Set.Icc a b ∨
            I = Set.Ioc a b ∨
            I = Set.Ico a b) →
        (∀ x ∈ I, g x ≤ f x) →
        ∀ x ∈ I, g x + h x ≤ f x + h x) := by
  sorry

end Chapter11

end TaoBench

/-- Mathlib translation of `Chapter3.SetTheory.Set.card_image_inj`.

If `α` is a finite type and `f : α → β` is injective, then the
(junk-extended-to-`ℕ`) cardinality of the image of `f` equals that of `α`.

Here `Cardinal.mk` is the cardinality of a type, and `Cardinal.toNat` sends a
cardinal to its finite size when finite, and to `0` when infinite, matching
the behavior of Tao's `Set.card`. -/
theorem card_image_inj
  {α β : Type*} [Fintype α] {f : α → β}
  (hf : Function.Injective f) :
  Cardinal.toNat (Cardinal.mk (Set.range f)) =
    Cardinal.toNat (Cardinal.mk α) := by
  sorry

end Set
end SetTheory

end TaoBench

/-- Theorem 11.4.1(c) / Exercise 11.4.1, translated to Mathlib:
If `f` and `g` are integrable on a bounded interval `I ⊆ ℝ`, then `f - g` is
integrable on `I`, and the integral of `f - g` over `I` is the difference of the
integrals of `f` and `g` over `I`. Here a \"bounded interval\" means a set of the
form `Icc a b`, `Ioc a b`, `Ico a b`, or `Ioo a b` for some real numbers
`a ≤ b`. -/
theorem IntegrableOn.sub
    {I : Set ℝ}
    (hI : ∃ a b : ℝ, a ≤ b ∧
      (I = Set.Icc a b ∨ I = Set.Ioc a b ∨ I = Set.Ico a b ∨ I = Set.Ioo a b))
    {f g : ℝ → ℝ}
    (hf : MeasureTheory.IntegrableOn f I)
    (hg : MeasureTheory.IntegrableOn g I) :
    MeasureTheory.IntegrableOn (f - g) I ∧
      (∫ x in I, (f - g) x) =
        (∫ x in I, f x) - (∫ x in I, g x) := by
  sorry

end TaoBench

/-- Mathlib translation of Tao's Chapter11.IntegrableOn.of_extend.
If a real-valued function `f` is integrable on a bounded interval `I`,
then its extension by zero outside `I` is integrable on any larger
bounded interval `J` containing `I`.
Here `I` and `J` are sets known to be one of the four standard
bounded real intervals: `(a,b)`, `[a,b]`, `(a,b]`, or `[a,b)`.
This uses Mathlib's `IntegrableOn` (Lebesgue integrability) on sets. -/
theorem IntegrableOn.of_extend
  {I J : Set ℝ}
  (hI : ∃ a b : ℝ,
    I = Ioo a b ∨ I = Ioc a b ∨ I = Ico a b ∨ I = Icc a b)
  (hJ : ∃ a b : ℝ,
    J = Ioo a b ∨ J = Ioc a b ∨ J = Ico a b ∨ J = Icc a b)
  (hIJ : I ⊆ J)
  {f : ℝ → ℝ}
  (h : IntegrableOn f I) :
  IntegrableOn (I.indicator f) J := by
  sorry

end TaoBench

For any partially ordered type `X`, there exists a subset `M : Set X` which is

      Maximal
        (fun S : Set X =>

/--
Tao, Analysis I, Thm. 11.4.1(h) (laws of integration), rephrased in Mathlib terms.

If `f` is integrable on a closed, bounded interval `[a, c]` and `b` is an
intermediate point (`a ≤ b ≤ c`), then `f` is integrable on `[a, b]` and `[b, c]`,
and the integral over `[a, c]` is the sum of the integrals over the two subintervals.
Here we use the (Lebesgue) set integral with respect to `volume`.
-/
theorem IntegrableOn.join
  {a b c : ℝ} {f : ℝ → ℝ}
  (hab : a ≤ b) (hbc : b ≤ c)
  (h : MeasureTheory.IntegrableOn f (Icc a c)) :
  MeasureTheory.IntegrableOn f (Icc a b) ∧
  MeasureTheory.IntegrableOn f (Icc b c) ∧
  (∫ x in Icc a c, f x) =
    (∫ x in Icc a b, f x) + (∫ x in Icc b c, f x) := by
  sorry

end TaoBench

/--
Tao's Proposition 11.5.6 / Exercise 11.5.1, translated to Mathlib:

A bounded, piecewise continuous real-valued function on a bounded real interval
is integrable (here: Lebesgue–Bochner interval integrable on `[a, b]`).

- `hbound` says `f` is bounded on the closed interval `Icc a b`.
- `hf` says there is a finite, strictly increasing sequence of points from `a`
  to `b` such that `f` is continuous on each closed subinterval between
  consecutive partition points.
- The conclusion is that `f` is `IntervalIntegrable` on `[a, b]` with respect
  to Lebesgue measure.
-/
theorem integ_of_bdd_piecewise_cts {a b : ℝ} {f : ℝ → ℝ}
  (hbound : ∃ M : ℝ, ∀ x ∈ Icc a b, |f x| ≤ M)
  (hf :
    ∃ (n : ℕ) (x : Fin (n + 1) → ℝ),
      x 0 = a ∧
      x (Fin.last n) = b ∧
      StrictMono x ∧
      ∀ (i : Fin n),
        ContinuousOn f (Icc (x (Fin.castSucc i)) (x i.succ)))
  : IntervalIntegrable f MeasureTheory.volume a b := by
  sorry

end TaoBench

theorem PiecewiseConstantOn.RS_integ_sub
  {α : StieltjesFunction} {s : Set ℝ}
  {f g : ℝ → ℝ}
  -- `s` is a bounded interval of one of the four usual types
  (hs : ∃ a b, s = Set.Icc a b ∨ s = Set.Ioc a b ∨ s = Set.Ico a b ∨ s = Set.Ioo a b)
  -- `f` and `g` are piecewise constant on `s` in the sense of having finite range there
  (hf_pw : (f '' s).Finite)
  (hg_pw : (g '' s).Finite)
  -- integrability of `f` and `g` on `s` for the Stieltjes measure associated to `α`
  (hf : MeasureTheory.IntegrableOn f s α.measure)
  (hg : MeasureTheory.IntegrableOn g s α.measure) :
  (∫ x in s, (f x - g x) ∂α.measure)
    = (∫ x in s, f x ∂α.measure)
      - (∫ x in s, g x ∂α.measure) := by
  sorry

end TaoBench

namespace TaoBench

/--
A Mathlib-style reformulation of Tao's Theorem 11.8.8(e): monotonicity of the
piecewise-constant Riemann–Stieltjes integral.

Here we work with a fixed finite partition of a closed interval
`[a 0, a (Fin.last n)]`, given by a strictly increasing sequence of points
`a : Fin (n.succ) → ℝ`. On each subinterval
`[a (Fin.castSucc i), a (Fin.succ i)]`, the functions `f` and `g` are assumed
to be constant. The “Riemann–Stieltjes integral” along this partition is the
finite sum
`∑ i, f (a (Fin.castSucc i)) * (α (a (Fin.succ i)) - α (a (Fin.castSucc i)))`,
and similarly for `g`.

Under these hypotheses, if `α` is monotone and `f ≤ g` on the whole interval,
then the corresponding sums satisfy the same inequality.
-/
theorem PiecewiseConstantOn.RS_integ_mono
  {n : ℕ}
  {a : Fin (n.succ) → ℝ}
  {α f g : ℝ → ℝ}
  (hα : Monotone α)
  (ha_mono : StrictMono a)
  (hf :
    ∀ i : Fin n,
      ∀ x ∈ Set.Icc (a (Fin.castSucc i)) (a (Fin.succ i)),
      ∀ y ∈ Set.Icc (a (Fin.castSucc i)) (a (Fin.succ i)),
        f x = f y)
  (hg :
    ∀ i : Fin n,
      ∀ x ∈ Set.Icc (a (Fin.castSucc i)) (a (Fin.succ i)),
      ∀ y ∈ Set.Icc (a (Fin.castSucc i)) (a (Fin.succ i)),
        g x = g y)
  (h :
    ∀ x ∈ Set.Icc (a 0) (a (Fin.last n)), f x ≤ g x) :
  (∑ i : Fin n,
      f (a (Fin.castSucc i)) *
        (α (a (Fin.succ i)) - α (a (Fin.castSucc i))))
    ≤
  (∑ i : Fin n,
      g (a (Fin.castSucc i)) *
        (α (a (Fin.succ i)) - α (a (Fin.castSucc i)))) := by
  sorry

end TaoBench

/-- Tao, Lemma 9.9.7 / Exercise 9.9.1, reformulated in Mathlib terms.

Two real-valued ℤ-indexed sequences `a` and `b` are equivalent (in Tao's sense)
iff their difference tends to `0` along `atTop`.  Here, equivalence is expressed
directly by the ε–N condition: for every `ε > 0` there is an index `N` such that
for all `n ≥ N` the distance between `a n` and `b n` is at most `ε`. -/
theorem equiv_iff (a b : ℤ → ℝ) :
    (∀ ε > (0 : ℝ), ∃ N : ℤ, ∀ n ≥ N, dist (a n) (b n) ≤ ε) ↔
      atTop.Tendsto (fun n : ℤ => a n - b n) (nhds 0) := by
  sorry

end Sequence
end Chapter6

/-- Proposition 9.9.8 / Exercise 9.9.2, using only Mathlib primitives.

`f` is uniformly continuous on a set `X ⊆ ℝ` if and only if it preserves the
following equivalence relation on real sequences taking values in `X`:

Two sequences `x, y : ℕ → ℝ` are *equivalent* if for every `ε > 0` there exists
`N : ℕ` such that for all `n ≥ N` we have `dist (x n) (y n) ≤ ε`. -/
theorem Chapter9.UniformContinuousOn.iff_preserves_equiv {X : Set ℝ} (f : ℝ → ℝ) :
  UniformContinuousOn f X ↔
    ∀ x y : ℕ → ℝ,
      (∀ n, x n ∈ X) →
      (∀ n, y n ∈ X) →
      (∀ ε > (0 : ℝ), ∃ N : ℕ, ∀ n ≥ N, dist (x n) (y n) ≤ ε) →
      (∀ ε > (0 : ℝ), ∃ N : ℕ, ∀ n ≥ N, dist (f (x n)) (f (y n)) ≤ ε) :=
by
  sorry

end TaoBench

/-- Mathlib translation of `Chapter11.PiecewiseConstantOn.RS_of_extend`.

Here `I` and `J` are (bounded) real intervals, expressed using the
standard interval sets `Set.Ioo`, `Set.Icc`, `Set.Ioc`, `Set.Ico`.

`h` says that `f` is piecewise constant on `I` with respect to some
finite partition of `I` into bounded intervals (again of the four
standard types), which are pairwise disjoint and whose union is `I`.

The conclusion says that there is a similar finite interval partition of
`J` on which the extended function `I.indicator f` is piecewise
constant. -/
theorem PiecewiseConstantOn.RS_of_extend
  {I J : Set ℝ}
  (hI : ∃ a b : ℝ,
    I = Set.Ioo a b ∨ I = Set.Icc a b ∨ I = Set.Ioc a b ∨ I = Set.Ico a b)
  (hJ : ∃ a b : ℝ,
    J = Set.Ioo a b ∨ J = Set.Icc a b ∨ J = Set.Ioc a b ∨ J = Set.Ico a b)
  (hIJ : I ⊆ J)
  {f : ℝ → ℝ}
  (h : ∃ P : Finset (Set ℝ),
      (∀ K ∈ P,
        (∃ a b : ℝ,
          K = Set.Ioo a b ∨ K = Set.Icc a b ∨ K = Set.Ioc a b ∨ K = Set.Ico a b) ∧
        K ⊆ I) ∧
      (∀ x, x ∈ I → ∃! K, K ∈ P ∧ x ∈ K) ∧
      (∀ K ∈ P, ∃ c, ∀ x ∈ K, f x = c))
  {α : ℝ → ℝ} (hα : Monotone α) :
  ∃ Q : Finset (Set ℝ),
    (∀ K ∈ Q,
      (∃ a b : ℝ,
        K = Set.Ioo a b ∨ K = Set.Icc a b ∨ K = Set.Ioc a b ∨ K = Set.Ico a b) ∧
      K ⊆ J) ∧
    (∀ x, x ∈ J → ∃! K, K ∈ Q ∧ x ∈ K) ∧
    (∀ K ∈ Q, ∃ c, ∀ x ∈ K, I.indicator f x = c) := by sorry

end TaoBench

/--
Mathlib translation of Tao's Proposition 6.4.12(c) / Exercise 6.4.3.

Let `a : ℤ → ℝ` be an integer-indexed real sequence which vanishes before some
index `m : ℤ`, i.e. `a n = 0` for all `n < m`.

Define, using extended reals `EReal`:

* `inf` as the infimum of the set of all values `a n` with `n ≥ m`;
* for each `N : ℤ`, the infimum of the tail `{a n | n ≥ N}`;
* `liminf` as the supremum over all `N ≥ m` of these tail infima.

Then `inf ≤ liminf`.
-/
theorem inf_le_liminf
    (m : ℤ) (a : ℤ → ℝ)
    (hvanish : ∀ n < m, a n = 0) :
    sInf {x : EReal | ∃ n : ℤ, n ≥ m ∧ x = a n} ≤
      sSup {x : EReal | ∃ N : ℤ, N ≥ m ∧
        x = sInf {y : EReal | ∃ n : ℤ, n ≥ N ∧ y = a n}} := by
  sorry

end Sequence

end TaoBench

/--
Exercise 7.1.4 (Tao, Analysis I, §7.1): a binomial theorem expansion with
coefficients written using factorials and the sum indexed by integers.
-/
theorem binomial_theorem (x y : ℝ) (n : ℕ) :
    (x + y)^n
      = ∑ j ∈ Icc (0 : ℤ) n,
          (n.factorial : ℝ) /
            ((j.toNat.factorial : ℝ) * ((n - j).toNat.factorial : ℝ)) *
            x^j * y^(n - j) := by
  sorry

end Finset

end TaoBench

/-- Exercise 7.1.6 (Mathlib formulation).
If a finite type `S` is written as a disjoint union of finitely many finite
subsets `E i`, then the sum of `f : S → ℝ` over all of `S` equals the
iterated sum over the pieces. -/
theorem sum_union_disjoint {n : ℕ} {S : Type*} [Fintype S]
    (E : Fin n → Finset S)
    (disj : ∀ i j : Fin n, i ≠ j → Disjoint (E i) (E j))
    (cover : ∀ s : S, ∃ i, s ∈ E i)
    (f : S → ℝ) :
    ∑ s, f s = ∑ i, ∑ s ∈ E i, f s := by
  sorry

    ContinuousWithinAt f (Icc a b) x₀ := by
  sorry

/-- Lemma 6.4.13 (Comparison principle) / Exercise 6.4.4.

If two real-valued sequences on `ℤ` both vanish below their respective starting
indices `ma` and `mb`, have the same starting index `ma = mb`, and satisfy
`a n ≤ b n` for all `n ≥ ma`, then the supremum (in `EReal`) of the values
`a n` for `n ≥ ma` is less than or equal to the corresponding supremum for `b`. -/
theorem sup_mono
  {ma mb : ℤ} {a b : ℤ → ℝ}
  (ha_vanish : ∀ n < ma, a n = 0)
  (hb_vanish : ∀ n < mb, b n = 0)
  (hm : ma = mb)
  (hab : ∀ n ≥ ma, a n ≤ b n) :
  sSup {x : EReal | ∃ n ≥ ma, x = a n} ≤
    sSup {x : EReal | ∃ n ≥ mb, x = b n} := by
  sorry

end Sequence

end TaoBench

/-- Lemma 6.4.13 (Comparison principle) / Exercise 6.4.4, in Mathlib terms.

We work with functions `a b : ℤ → ℝ` together with an index `m : ℤ` such that
both sequences vanish for all `n < m`.  For such data, Tao's definition of
`liminf` is

* for each `N`, the lower limit of the tail starting at `max m N` is
  `sInf { y : EReal | ∃ n ≥ max m N, y = a n }`,
* and the liminf is the supremum over `N ≥ m` of these lower limits.

Under the pointwise comparison `a n ≤ b n` for all `n ≥ m`, the liminf of `a`
is bounded above by the liminf of `b`. -/
theorem liminf_mono
  {m : ℤ} {a b : ℤ → ℝ}
  (ha_vanish : ∀ n < m, a n = 0)
  (hb_vanish : ∀ n < m, b n = 0)
  (hab : ∀ n ≥ m, a n ≤ b n) :
    sSup { x : EReal | ∃ N ≥ m,
      x = sInf { y : EReal | ∃ n ≥ max m N, y = a n } }
    ≤
    sSup { x : EReal | ∃ N ≥ m,
      x = sInf { y : EReal | ∃ n ≥ max m N, y = b n } } := by
  sorry

end Sequence

end TaoBench

/-- Exercise 10.2.6 (Tao, Analysis I).

If a real function is continuous on `[a, b]`, differentiable on `(a, b)`, and its
derivative on `(a, b)` is bounded in absolute value by `M > 0`, then it is
Lipschitz with constant `M` on `(a, b)`. -/
theorem lipschitz_bound
  {M a b : ℝ} (hM : M > 0) (hab : a < b) {f : ℝ → ℝ}
  (hcont : ContinuousOn f (Icc a b))
  (hderiv : DifferentiableOn ℝ f (Ioo a b))
  (hlip : ∀ x ∈ Ioo a b, |derivWithin f (Ioo a b) x| ≤ M)

  |f x - f y| ≤ M * |x - y| := by

namespace TaoBench

/--
Mathlib-only version of Tao's Exercise 6.4.8:

For a real sequence `u : ℕ → ℝ`, let `L` be its extended-real limsup
`Filter.limsup (fun n ↦ (u n : EReal)) atTop`. Then `L` is an
“extended limit point” of `u` in Tao's sense: if `L = +∞` then `u` is
not bounded above; if `L = -∞` then `u` is not bounded below; and if
`L` is finite then the real number underlying `L` is a limit point of
`u` in the usual ε–N sense.
-/
theorem Sequence.extended_limit_point_of_limsup (u : ℕ → ℝ) :
  (let L : EReal :=
    Filter.limsup (fun n : ℕ => (u n : EReal)) (Filter.atTop : Filter ℕ);
    if hL : L = ⊤ then
      -- case `L = +∞`: `u` is not bounded above
      ¬ ∃ M : ℝ, ∀ n : ℕ, u n ≤ M
    else if hL' : L = ⊥ then
      -- case `L = -∞`: `u` is not bounded below
      ¬ ∃ M : ℝ, ∀ n : ℕ, u n ≥ M
    else
      -- finite case: `L.toReal` is a limit point of `u`
      ∀ ε > (0 : ℝ), ∀ N : ℕ, ∃ n ≥ N, dist (u n) L.toReal ≤ ε) := by
  sorry

end TaoBench

namespace Sequence

/-- Mathlib translation of Tao's `Sequence.extended_limit_point_of_liminf`.

For a real sequence `u : ℕ → ℝ`, let
`L := Filter.liminf (fun n : ℕ => (u n : EReal)) atTop`.

The statement `a.ExtendedLimitPoint a.liminf` in Tao's setting becomes:

* If `L = ⊤`, then `u` is not bounded above.
* If `L = ⊥`, then `u` is not bounded below.
* If `L` is finite, then `L.toReal` is a limit point of `u`
  in the sense that every tail of `u` comes arbitrarily close to `L.toReal`. -/
theorem extended_limit_point_of_liminf (u : ℕ → ℝ) :
  (if
      Filter.liminf (fun n : ℕ => (u n : EReal)) (Filter.atTop : Filter ℕ)
        = (⊤ : EReal)
    then
      ¬ ∃ M : ℝ, ∀ n : ℕ, u n ≤ M
    else
      if
        Filter.liminf (fun n : ℕ => (u n : EReal)) (Filter.atTop : Filter ℕ)
          = (⊥ : EReal)
      then
        ¬ ∃ M : ℝ, ∀ n : ℕ, u n ≥ M
      else
        ∀ ε > (0 : ℝ), ∀ N : ℕ, ∃ n ≥ N,
          dist (u n)
               (EReal.toReal
                 (Filter.liminf (fun n : ℕ => (u n : EReal))
                   (Filter.atTop : Filter ℕ)))
            ≤ ε
  ) := by
  sorry

end Sequence

end TaoBench

/-- There exists a real sequence whose extended limit points (in `EReal`) are
exactly `⊥`, `0`, and `⊤`.

Here an extended limit point `L : EReal` of a sequence `a : ℕ → ℝ` means:
* if `L = ⊤`, then `a` is not bounded above;
* if `L = ⊥`, then `a` is not bounded below;
* if `L` is finite, then for every `ε > 0` and every `N` there exists `n ≥ N`
  with `dist (a n) (EReal.toReal L) ≤ ε`. -/
theorem exists_three_limit_points :
  ∃ a : ℕ → ℝ,
    ∀ L : EReal,
      (if L = ⊤ then
          ¬ ∃ M : ℝ, ∀ n : ℕ, a n ≤ M
        else if L = ⊥ then
          ¬ ∃ M : ℝ, ∀ n : ℕ, M ≤ a n
        else
          ∀ ε > (0 : ℝ),
            ∀ N : ℕ, ∃ n ≥ N, dist (a n) (EReal.toReal L) ≤ ε)
      ↔
      L = ⊥ ∨ L = 0 ∨ L = ⊤ := by
  sorry

end Sequence

end Chapter6

/-- Exercise 6.4.10 (Mathlib version).

If every term of a sequence `b` (from some index onward) is a limit point of a sequence `a`,
and if `c` is a limit point of `b`, then `c` is a limit point of `a`.

Here, a real number `x` is a *limit point* of a real sequence `s : ℤ → ℝ` with respect to a lower
bound `m : ℤ` if for every `ε > 0` and every `N ≥ m` there exists `n ≥ N` with `dist (s n) x ≤ ε`. -/
theorem limit_points_of_limit_points
    {a b : ℤ → ℝ} {ma mb : ℤ} {c : ℝ}
    (hab :
      ∀ {n : ℤ}, n ≥ mb →
        ∀ {ε : ℝ}, ε > 0 →
          ∀ {N : ℤ}, N ≥ ma →
            ∃ n' : ℤ, n' ≥ N ∧ dist (a n') (b n) ≤ ε)
    (hbc :
      ∀ {ε : ℝ}, ε > 0 →
        ∀ {N : ℤ}, N ≥ mb →
          ∃ n : ℤ, n ≥ N ∧ dist (b n) c ≤ ε) :
    ∀ {ε : ℝ}, ε > 0 →
      ∀ {N : ℤ}, N ≥ ma →
        ∃ n : ℤ, n ≥ N ∧ dist (a n) c ≤ ε :=
  by sorry

end Sequence

end TaoBench

/-- Exercise 6.1.10 (Mathlib-only reformulation).

For sequences `a b : ℕ → ℚ`, the following are equivalent:

* For every `ε > 0`, there exists an integer `N` such that for all integers
  `n ≥ max 0 N`, the extended sequences
  `n ↦ if 0 ≤ n then a n.toNat else 0` and
  `n ↦ if 0 ≤ n then b n.toNat else 0`
  are `ε`-close: `|a n.toNat - b n.toNat| ≤ ε`.

* For every `ε > 0`, there exists a natural number `N` such that for all
  `n ≥ N` we have `|a n - b n| ≤ ε`. -/
theorem Sequence.equiv_rat (a b : ℕ → ℚ) :
    (∀ ε > (0 : ℚ),
      ∃ N : ℤ,
        ∀ n : ℤ, n ≥ max (0 : ℤ) N →
          |a n.toNat - b n.toNat| ≤ ε) ↔
    (∀ ε > (0 : ℚ),
      ∃ N : ℕ,
        ∀ n ≥ N, |a n - b n| ≤ ε) := by
  sorry

end Chapter5

  sorry

namespace Chapter3

/-- Mathlib translation of Tao, Exercise 3.4.7.

In Tao's ZF-style setting, for sets `X` and `Y` of objects there is a set `Z`
whose elements are exactly the *partial functions* from `X` to `Y`, where a
partial function is a (total) function from some subset of `X` to some subset
of `Y`.

Here we work in type theory with a base type `α` of "objects", and actual sets
are subsets `X Y : Set α`. A *partial function from `X` to `Y`* is represented
as a function `f : α → Option α` whose domain of definition is contained in `X`
and whose values always lie in `Y`. The theorem asserts that the collection of
such partial functions forms a set. -/
theorem SetTheory.Set.partial_functions
  {α : Type*} {X Y : Set α} :
  ∃ Z : Set (α → Option α),
    ∀ f : α → Option α,
      f ∈ Z ↔
        (∀ ⦃x⦄, x ∉ X → f x = none) ∧
        (∀ ⦃x y⦄, f x = some y → y ∈ Y) := by
  sorry

end Chapter3

end TaoBench

theorem PiecewiseConstantOn.RS_integ_of_comp
  {a b : ℝ} (hab : a < b) {phi f : ℝ → ℝ}
  (hphi_cont : Continuous phi) (hphi_mono : Monotone phi)
  (hf : ∃ n : ℕ,
    ∃ c : Fin n → ℝ,
    ∃ pts : Fin (n+1) → ℝ,
      StrictMono pts ∧
      pts 0 = phi a ∧
      pts (Fin.last n) = phi b ∧
      ∀ i : Fin n, ∀ x ∈ Set.Icc (pts (Fin.castSucc i)) (pts (Fin.succ i)),
        f x = c i) :
  (∃ n : ℕ,
    ∃ c : Fin n → ℝ,
    ∃ pts : Fin (n+1) → ℝ,
      StrictMono pts ∧
      pts 0 = a ∧
      pts (Fin.last n) = b ∧
      ∀ i : Fin n, ∀ x ∈ Set.Icc (pts (Fin.castSucc i)) (pts (Fin.succ i)),
        (f (phi x)) = c i) ∧
  (∫ x in a..b, f (phi x) ∂((  
      { toFun := phi,
        mono' := hphi_mono,
        right_continuous' := by
          intro x
          have hx : ContinuousAt phi x := hphi_cont.continuousAt
          simpa using hx.continuousWithinAt
      } : StieltjesFunction).measure) =
    ∫ y in (phi a)..(phi b), f y) := by sorry

/--
Mathlib version of Tao's Proposition 8.2.6(a) / Exercise 8.2.3:

For functions `f g : X → ℝ` such that the families of absolute values `|f|` and `|g|`
are summable (i.e. the series `∑' x, |f x|` and `∑' x, |g x|` converge),
the family `|f + g|` is also summable, and the (unconditionally defined) infinite sum
is additive:
`∑' x, (f x + g x) = (∑' x, f x) + (∑' x, g x)`.

Here `Summable (fun x => |f x|)` is the Mathlib equivalent of Tao's
`AbsConvergent' f`, and `∑' x, f x` is the Mathlib equivalent of Tao's `Sum' f`.
-/
theorem Sum'.add {X : Type*} {f g : X → ℝ}
    (hf : Summable fun x => |f x|)
    (hg : Summable fun x => |g x|) :
    Summable (fun x => |f x + g x|) ∧
      (∑' x, (f x + g x)) = (∑' x, f x) + (∑' x, g x) := by
  sorry

end TaoBench

namespace Chapter9

/-- Exercise 9.8.4 (Mathlib translation).

Given real numbers `a < b` and a function `f : ℝ → ℝ` that is continuous and
monotone on the closed interval `[a, b]`, this definition asks for a decision
(on the classical `Decidable` type) of the following statement:

1. The image of `[a, b]` under `f` is exactly the closed interval
   `[f a, f b]`, and

2. There exists a function `finv : ℝ → ℝ` such that:
   * `finv` is continuous on `[f a, f b]`,
   * `finv` is strictly monotone on `[f a, f b]`,
   * the image of `[f a, f b]` under `finv` is exactly `[a, b]`,
   * `finv` is a left inverse of `f` on `[a, b]`, i.e. `finv (f x) = x`
     for all `x ∈ [a, b]`, and
   * `finv` is a right inverse of `f` on `[f a, f b]`, i.e. `f (finv y) = y`
     for all `y ∈ [f a, f b]`. -/
def MonotoneOn.exist_inverse_without_strictmono
  {a b : ℝ} (h : a < b) (f : ℝ → ℝ)
  (hcont : ContinuousOn f (Set.Icc a b))
  (hmono : MonotoneOn f (Set.Icc a b)) :
  Decidable
    (f '' (Set.Icc a b) = Set.Icc (f a) (f b) ∧
      ∃ finv : ℝ → ℝ,
        ContinuousOn finv (Set.Icc (f a) (f b)) ∧
        StrictMonoOn finv (Set.Icc (f a) (f b)) ∧
        finv '' (Set.Icc (f a) (f b)) = Set.Icc a b ∧
        (∀ x ∈ Set.Icc a b, finv (f x) = x) ∧
        ∀ y ∈ Set.Icc (f a) (f b), f (finv y) = y) :=
by
  sorry

end Chapter9
end TaoBench

/-- Mathlib translation of Tao's Proposition 6.3.6 / Exercise 6.3.2.

Tao's `Sequence` (Def. 6.1.3) is an integer-indexed real sequence `seq : ℤ → ℝ`
with a base index `m : ℤ` such that `seq n = 0` for all `n < m`. His `sup`
(Def. 6.3.1) is the supremum in `EReal` of the tail values `seq n` for `n ≥ m`.

Here we inline that structure: `a : ℤ → ℝ` is the sequence, `m : ℤ` is the base
index, and `hvanish` encodes the vanishing condition below `m`. -/
theorem Sequence.le_sup
    {a : ℤ → ℝ} {m n : ℤ}
    (hvanish : ∀ k < m, a k = 0) (hn : n ≥ m) :
    (a n : EReal) ≤ sSup { x : EReal | ∃ k ≥ m, x = (a k : EReal) } := by
  sorry

end TaoBench

variable {α : Type*} {n : ℕ}

/-- Equality of `n`-tuples (functions `Fin n → α`) is pointwise equality. -/
theorem eq (t t' : Fin n → α) :
    t = t' ↔ ∀ i : Fin n, t i = t' i := by
  sorry

/-- Exercise 11.6.4 (Mathlib formulation).

There exists a function `f : ℝ → ℝ` which is nonnegative on `[0, ∞)`, whose
(infinite) sum over `ℝ` is summable, but for which the integrals over the
intervals `[0, N]` are not uniformly bounded above as `N → ∞`. -/
theorem Exercise_11_6_4a :
  ∃ (f : ℝ → ℝ) (hnon : ∀ x ≥ 0, 0 ≤ f x),
    Summable f ∧
    ¬ ∃ M, ∀ N ≥ 0, ∫ x in 0..N, f x ≤ M := by
  sorry

end TaoBench
